system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

Pages 242-311 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

  Chapter 7                                                                                            4668




  Leading Tone and How Other Scales Appeared
  Going back in time a little, we understand how some variations of the Natural Minor Scale and
  Natural Major Scale appeared, which later generated other Harmonic Fields in addition to the
  two we already know. For such, we must understand what "leading tone" means: A leading tone
  is what we call the note that resolves (or leads to) another tone a half step above or a half step
  below it. However, functionally speaking it is the VII degree of the diatonic scale (Natural
  Major Scale). Therefore, the note "B" is the leading tone of the "C" note which gives us the
  feeling of tension and preparation for this tone. The discovery of the scales began in the
  monasteries of the Middle Ages, where all religious events were held with music sung by the
  monks, later named "Gregorian Chants". One of the greatest musical problems for the church
  choir at the time was to be able to sing the Natural Minor Scale in tune and this happened for
  one reason: the Natural Minor Scale had no leading key.


  "A" natural minor
                                œ      œ       œ      œ       œ      œ       œ
             œ     œ     œ                                                           œ   œ   œ
      &œ                                                                                         œ

  So, to end this problem, the VII degree of the Natural Minor Scale was changed half a step up,
  forming a scale as complete as the Natural Major Scale because now it has a Leading Tone. That
                                 68
  scale was later called the Harmonic Minor Scale.

  "A" harmonic minor
             œ     œ
                       46œ      œ      œ     #œ       œ     #œ       œ       œ       œ   œ   œ
      &œ                                                                                         œ

  In addition to the Harmonic Minor Scale, another scale was created: the Melodic Minor Scale.
  Initially, that scale was used only in its ascending order, because the descending order was
  difficult to sing in tune, as it sounded a lot like the Natural Major Scale, and contained a minor
  3rd at the end. So it was used this way:


  "A" melodic minor
                                œ     #œ     #œ       œ     #œ      #œ       œ
             œ     œ     œ                                                           œ   œ   œ
      &œ                                                                                         œ

223                          2019 © Jupiter Musical Group Ltd. All Rights Reserved


It is very important to remember that the Melodic Minor Scale was played differently in its
                                                                                                    4668




descending order due to a human deficiency of not being able to sing it, rather than to a musical
issue. Obviously this deficiency came to an end with musical development. And it was a few
centuries later that it began to be widely used in its ascending and descending form and shape by
composer Johann Sebastian Bach, thus being named the "Bach Scale".
Currently the Melodic Minor Scale is considered by most modern authors as being equal to the
Bach Scale, meaning that it has only one form, both ascending and descending.

melodic minor scale / bach scale

In the mid-twentieth century, the Russian composer Nicolai A. Rimsky-Korsakov, named
another variation of the Natural Major Scale: Harmonic Major Scale

harmonic major scale
 & œ                          œ     bœ       œ      œ       œ     bœ       œ
           œ     œ     œ                                                           œ   œ   œ   œ
Therefore, we have learned two Natural Minor Scale variations and one Natural Major Scale
variation, totaling three new scales:

1) Harmonic Minor Scale
2) Melodic Minor Scale
                               68
3) Harmonic Major Scale
                     46
Just like the natural major and natural minor scale, each one of these three new scales generate
seven different modes, creating three groups of seven scales each. In theory, we could also
consider them harmonic fields, however in order to avoid confusion with names, we will call
them"scale modes".

It is important to understant that we cannot use the chord families derived from these three
groups of scale modes to guide us through songs, we can only use them to add more "colors" to
the chords. Next we will learn the three groups of scale modes and their chord families:

Studing the Melodic Minor Chord family                                                                 4668




Study Devices:
a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.

b) Always study with rhythm (bossa nova, swing, funk,etc). It helps you to memorize things
   faster.

c) Before you change or add a new key, make sure you know the ones you practiced
   previously.

d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only
   the 6th and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be studied only when you are
   comfortable with the others.

Studing the Harmonic Major Chord family

Study Devices:
a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.

b) Always study with rhythm (funk, swing, etc). It helps you to memorize things faster.

c) Before you change or add a new key, make sure you know the ones you practiced
   previously.

d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only the
   6th and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be studied only after you are
   comfortable with the others.
